reviewed.by: New reel on Bible contradictions. apologia-citations = 0 fabrications (one-angel [Matt 28/Mark 16] vs two [Luke 24/John 20] harmonization correct). argument+orthodoxy via general-purpose running each agent's .md = orthodoxy CLEAN (no manuscript->truth overreach, denominationally neutral, no strict-inerrancy adjudication); argument WEAK->fixed the key precision slip: the reel discusses NARRATIVE discrepancies but had slid into textual-criticism 'variant' language + 'catalogued every variant' (borrows the Comma-Johanneum-adjacent manuscript debate's authority) -> changed to 'scholars have studied them for centuries' and 'none of them PUTS a core teaching in doubt' (vs 'touch', which the 1 John 5:7 interpolation would falsify). The honest 'a few passages are genuinely hard' concession kept intact so the car-crash analogy doesn't over-prove.

voiceover: Isn't the Bible full of contradictions? Fewer than you've been told. Picture a car crash with four witnesses. One saw the color, one heard the horn, one counted the passengers — each notices different things, and none of them is lying. That's the Gospels. One writer mentions the angel at the tomb who spoke; another counts two who were there. Both can be true. Complementary details aren't contradictions — they're what real testimony looks like. And it cuts the other way: if four accounts matched word for word, you'd rightly suspect they'd been rehearsed. Now, be honest — a few passages are genuinely hard, and scholars have studied them for centuries. But none of them puts a single core teaching of the faith in doubt. The differences are minor. The event they all report — a man raised from the dead — is not.
